Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually study in bursts when I could find time between my part-time job and other commitments. I preferred listening to recorded lectures while commuting or doing chores since I learn better by hearing things. With limited resources, I had to be creative about when and where I could actually sit down and focus on coursework.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what shifts I was working that week. I couldn't really maintain a consistent schedule because my job hours changed constantly. When deadlines approached, I'd cram in as many study hours as possible, but otherwise it was pretty irregular. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then other weeks I'd put in serious time.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I remembered an assignment was coming up or when I got a notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything new I needed to worry about. It wasn't really a habit for me—more like a reactive thing when I felt like I might be missing something important.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my daily routine. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more regularly, but then I'd fall off again. The gamified tasks they introduced were kind of interesting and made me log in a few extra times, but it never stuck as a consistent habit.
Q5: The audio lectures and any video content were the most useful for me since I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them while doing other things, which helped me maximize my limited time. The discussion forums were okay too—sometimes hearing other students explain concepts in different ways helped things click for me.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and text-heavy resources. With my learning style, reading dense PDFs just didn't work well for me, and I'd zone out. I also skipped a lot of the optional supplementary stuff because I simply didn't have the time or energy to engage with things that weren't directly required.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly connected to an assignment or exam, or if it presented information in a way I could actually absorb—like audio or video. If something felt repetitive or like busy work, I'd lose interest fast. I needed to see the point of doing something, especially given how stretched thin I already was.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones I'd do because I had to, even if I was bored or tired. With optional stuff, I'd think about whether I had the energy and usually the answer was no. It's not that I didn't see their value—I just had to prioritize survival over enrichment.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of audio files and listened to them offline on my phone during commutes and breaks at work. I also borrowed textbooks from friends since I couldn't always afford my own materials. A lot of my studying happened with headphones in while I was doing something else entirely.
Q10: Most of my learning happened while I was listening to downloaded lectures during my commute or while working my job. The LMS would show I accessed something once, but I'd replay those audio files dozens of times. My actual engagement was way higher than what any login data would suggest.
Q11: Yeah, constantly. I'd download the lecture recordings or any audio content and then never go back to the LMS for that material again. I'd listen to the same recording multiple times over several days, but the system would only register that one initial download. It was just more practical for my situation.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially when my work hours increased or when I was dealing with money stress. When you're worried about making rent, it's hard to care about discussion posts. The boredom also got to me sometimes—when content felt dry or irrelevant, my motivation just tanked completely.
Q13: Definitely. Fatigue was a big one—after long shifts, I'd try to log in but my brain was just mush. Sometimes I'd open the LMS and just stare at it without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities meant the course often got pushed to the back burner until deadlines forced my hand.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open while I was half-watching videos or scrolling through materials without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere—thinking about bills, work schedules, whatever. I was technically present but mentally checked out. It happened more than I'd like to admit.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they felt tedious and I was usually too tired to form coherent thoughts. I knew participating mattered for my grade, but I'd keep telling myself I'd do it later. Sometimes I avoided starting assignments because I was scared I wouldn't understand the material well enough.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to really engage. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the motivation to log in and actually do the work. Without that pressure, I'd drift. The gamified elements helped a bit by creating mini-deadlines, but real assignment due dates were what drove my behavior.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When I got encouraging feedback, it motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But when feedback was delayed or generic, it felt pointless. I needed to hear that my effort mattered, especially since I was putting in work under difficult circumstances.
Q18: The structure was okay, but sometimes the pacing didn't match my life at all. When multiple things were due at once, I'd get overwhelmed and end up doing everything poorly. I appreciated when there was flexibility, but rigid structures clashed with my unpredictable schedule pretty badly.
Q19: Having audio and video content made engagement way easier for me—that matched how I learn. But the heavy reliance on reading materials and text-based discussions made things harder. Also, the low interactivity sometimes made the whole experience feel isolating, which contributed to my boredom.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers or trying to understand the material but struggling to actually produce work. They could be confused, overwhelmed, or dealing with personal stuff that makes it hard to follow through. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just spinning wheels.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I didn't log in much, but I was studying offline constantly—listening to lectures on repeat, reviewing notes I'd written out. My exam scores weren't amazing, but I passed, and a lot of that learning happened completely outside the LMS. Low activity doesn't mean low effort.
Q22: Course logs can show when you accessed things and how often, but they miss the depth of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you actually understood something or just clicked through it. They definitely can't capture offline study, audio replays, or the mental effort you put in outside the platform.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm genuinely curious or invested in understanding something, not just trying to check a box. For me, real engagement happens when I'm listening to a lecture and suddenly something clicks.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I studied during my commutes, how many times I replayed audio lectures, or the mental energy I spent trying to balance everything. It wouldn't capture my stress about money or how hard I worked to pass despite everything working against me. The data only tells a fraction of the story.
